feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Three practical examples:

paragraphs.text: Feedback to the European Commission

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
paragraphs.text: We would like to thank you for the opportunity to comment on your proposal for a Digital Fairness Act for new and second-hand car dealers, brand-based authorised repairers and free repairers.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
paragraphs.text: The Central Association of German Motor Vehicle Industry (ZDK) in Bonn, Berlin and Brussels represents the professional interests of 39.230 car houses, car bodies and master craft manufacturers with 468.000 employees. In 2024, the companies organised at the ZDK generated a turnover of around EUR 224.2 billion from the sale of new and second-hand vehicles, as well as maintenance, repair and service. The ZDK, with over 98.500 apprentices in the craft sector, is a leader in training.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
A. General
paragraphs.text: Our sector, the backbone of individual mobility for millions of citizens, is dominated by small and medium-sized enterprises. It operates in a complex hybrid market, which lives from face-to-face contact in the car dealership and the garage, as well as the ever-increasing importance of digital channels such as its own dealer websites (e.g. service and repair contracts) and online vehicle exchanges.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
A. General
paragraphs.text: We therefore welcome in principle the Commission's objective of making the Digital Single Market fair, transparent and trustworthy for all actors. A harmonised legal framework can increase consumer confidence, prevent unfair practices and facilitate cross-border trade. Fair digital processes continue to increase consumer confidence, be it when buying a car or when booking a workshop appointment.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
A. General
paragraphs.text: Nevertheless, we see the proposal with some concerns and would like to draw attention to the specific challenges and realities of our sector. The intended regulation must not lead to disproportionate burdens and burdens for our predominantly medium-sized motor vehicle companies, jeopardise their competitiveness (rather than strengthen them) or undermine existing legal instruments.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
B. Opinion on the legislative act and in particular on sector-specific risks
1. Increasing complexity and red tape for SMEs
paragraphs.text: The example of the online sale of a vehicle shows that it is already regulated by a narrow set of rules. These include comprehensive information requirements under the Consumer Rights Directive, strict rules on material liability, the Car Energy Labelling Regulation (Pkw-EnVKV) and the complex rules on distance withdrawal.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
B. Opinion on the legislative act and in particular on sector-specific risks
1. Increasing complexity and red tape for SMEs
paragraphs.text: In addition, companies with the operational implementation and application of the requirements and requirements of the UCPD, the GDPR, the Digital Services Act and the Data Act are required.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
B. Opinion on the legislative act and in particular on sector-specific risks
1. Increasing complexity and red tape for SMEs
paragraphs.text: In addition to the general information obligations, a workshop faces the challenge of offering complex services online in a comprehensible and legally compliant manner.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
B. Opinion on the legislative act and in particular on sector-specific risks
1. Increasing complexity and red tape for SMEs
paragraphs.text: The DFA threatens to place another, complex level of regulation on the existing legal framework. For a medium-

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
B. Opinion on the legislative act and in particular on sector-specific risks
1. Increasing complexity and red tape for SMEs
paragraphs.text: sized car business without a large legal department, it is hardly easy to continuously adapt its own website, software tools and sales processes to new potentially overlapping EU rules (in addition to the DSA, the DMA, the Data Act now the DFA), or to check compliance with a new 'fairness architecture' that has hitherto been interpreted. The risks of liability and the risk of costly warnings for minor formal errors in the onlinepresentation would further increase for businesses.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
B. Opinion on the legislative act and in particular on sector-specific risks
1. Increasing complexity and red tape for SMEs
paragraphs.text: This is particularly the case where companies use standard third-party software (e.g. for online forward bookings, vehicle configurations or advertisements) and have no direct influence on their design.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Two practical examples:
paragraphs.text: A trader offering a new or second-hand vehicle online already has to provide a wealth of information: Vehicle details, final price including all taxes and transfer costs, warranty information, notice of withdrawal, etc. If the DFA now requires additional requirements for the 'architecture' of the website, the prohibition of certain design elements or the detailed disclosure of personalisation algorithms, this leads to significant additional costs for web design, IT service providers and an in-depth legal assessment.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Two practical examples:
paragraphs.text: A workshop uses a default plugin of a third-party supplier for the online forward booking. If this tool is now classified as 'manipulative', for example because it pre-selects a more expensive service option, the workshop operator is liable in case of doubt, even though it has no influence on the design of the tool.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Two practical examples:
paragraphs.text: => the liability here must lie with the technology provider, not with the applying SME.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Two practical examples:
paragraphs.text: Clearly and practically defining the requirements for the design of online shops, the presentation of information and the handling of personalised offers by the Commission could increase legal certainty for traders.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Two practical examples:
paragraphs.text: No view of digital fairness in isolation from a consumer perspective

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Two practical examples:
paragraphs.text: In our view, a 'digital fairness for the consumer' (B2C) cannot be considered in isolation, but is directly dependent in our industry on fair conditions between our businesses and the major players, in particular vehicle manufacturers and technology platforms (B2B). This concerns, in particular, free and branded repairers, which, as a rule, can already be freely chosen by the consumer.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
a) Free repairers
paragraphs.text: A consumer's decision to consult a free repairer depends heavily on its non-discriminatory access to vehicle-generated data, repair information (RMI) and spare parts, which is still hampered by, in particular, digital barriers from vehicle manufacturers.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
a) Free repairers
paragraphs.text: DFA should also classify as 'dark patterns' user interfaces (e.g. in the vehicle's infotainment system) that specifically prevent the vehicle owner from sharing his/her data with an independent repairer. The DFA must be consistent with and support the objectives of the Data Act. The freedom of choice of the workshop shall not be undermined by the manufacturer's unfair interface design.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
a) Free repairers
paragraphs.text: Many repairers are listed on booking or comparison portals (platforms). The DFA must ensure that the operators of these portals do not abuse their market power, e.g. by non-transparent ranking algorithms or by favouring their own or connected repair chains.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
a) Free repairers
paragraphs.text: We call on the Commission to also consider digital barriers on the part of car manufacturers as an unfair practice within the meaning of the DFA.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
a) Free repairers
paragraphs.text: For example, a user interface in the vehicle display, which specifically discourages the vehicle owner from doing so or makes it unnecessarily difficult for him to release data to an independent workshop, is a dark pattern. The DFA must make the consumer's data sovereignty rights enshrined in the Data Act enforceable in practice, rather than undermining them through unfair design.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
b) Branded repairers and distributors
paragraphs.text: Brand-linked businesses are always confronted with the fact that they are, on the one hand, self-employed and self-employed traders but, on the other hand, they are subject to the usually strict contractual requirements of manufacturers (e.g. CI guidelines, predetermined IT systems), which usually involve large amounts of investment.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
b) Branded repairers and distributors
paragraphs.text: DFA shall not create any legal obligations which conflict with these contractual obligations or which place brand-based dealers and repairers in an unresolvable wrapper between the expectations of the EU and those of the manufacturers contractually linked to them.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
b) Branded repairers and distributors
paragraphs.text: ☐ repairers and branded repairers are listed on booking or comparison portals (platforms). The DFA must ensure that the operators of these portals do not abuse their market power, for example through non-transparent ranking algorithms.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
4. Legal uncertainty due to unclear definitions
paragraphs.text: We welcome the intended ban on manipulative designs/practices and take the view that it protects reputable distributors and repairers from unfair competitors putting consumers under pressure online and ensures a more level playing field.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
4. Legal uncertainty due to unclear definitions
paragraphs.text: However, the terms used so far in the legal act, such as 'manipulative design', 'suitable interfaces' or 'unreasonable pressure', appear to us to be legally imprecise, creating considerable legal uncertainty for our businesses.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
4. Legal uncertainty due to unclear definitions
paragraphs.text: In particular, new and second-hand car dealers will be faced with the question of what constitutes a legitimate promotional measure and what is an unlawful dark pattern.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: a) In car trade, it is common to point to the limited availability of a certain new or second-hand car or to a temporary offer of financing. Is this inadmissible?

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: b) Does the mere indication to a consumer that a specific second-hand car is a unicat and that several interested parties have already observed or have made several requests to that effect constitute legitimate information to the consumer or already an inadmissible dark pattern?

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: c) Does a temporary action price in the workshop service in the autumn for the change of tyre constitute a fair advertising or as an unauthorised pressure?

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: => we fear that an overly broad interpretation could criminalise established, transparent and permitted sales practices and services offered by brand-based traders and repairers for decades. The boundary between permitted advertising (e.g. "weekly offer") and undue pressure (e.g. "only 1 vehicle available, 10 other users

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: seeing this car" must be clearly and unambiguously drawn in order to provide legal certainty.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: Without clear and practical positive and negative lists, we understand that the DFA will provide a gateway for litigation, which benefit mainly specialised dental laundries, but not consumers.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: Regulating personalisation

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: Car houses increasingly use data to make relevant offers to their customers (e.g. a Van family for a customer who has previously searched for such vehicles).

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The transparency requirements for personalisation algorithms must not go so far as to reveal trade secrets or be practically impracticable. A small trader often cannot explain in detail how a third-party software (e.g. for advertisements) works. Here, the responsibility must lie clearly with the technology provider and not be passed on to the user distributor.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: Overlap with existing law:

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: Consumer law (in particular the UCPD) already prohibits misleading and aggressive commercial practices. It must be ensured that the DFA does not contradict these well-established rules or lead to contradictory assessments of the same facts. Double regulation for the same facts must be avoided for the Commission.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: Specific challenges, in particular in vehicle trade and operational risks

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: We identified the following specific risks for our operations:

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: a) Trade in vehicles (new and second-hand)

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The sale of new and second-hand cars is fundamentally different from the sale of simple consumer goods.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The 14-day right of withdrawal from distance selling for a high-quality asset such as the car remains a key risk for traders and already poses immense problems. A vehicle, once registered and driven, suffers a significant loss of value, often four digits, which can hardly be fully compensated in the event of revocation. Judicial disputes concerning the compensation to be paid by the withdrawing consumer are often the subject of civil proceedings. Any additional formal requirement by the DFA increases the risk for traders of formal errors that the withdrawal period will unnoticed be extended to one year and 14 days. This threatens the existence of an asset such as a car.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: In the case of second-hand cars, the exact description of the condition (including pre-damage, wear, etc.) is decisive. A presentation of this complex information, if necessary in the context of a standardised and highly regulated online interface, would have to be carried out for second-hand car dealers only in time, which is then missing for the sales transaction. In our view, a fairness by design approach should not lead to important individual vehicle information falling into standardised masks.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: b) Workshop service (free and branded)

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The repair of a vehicle is a complex service. By its very nature, an estimate made online or by telephone and communicated to the consumer, based on the consumer's information, can only be a first rough estimate. The actual costs may change following a physical diagnosis at the workshop, because an accurate diagnose of damage is regularly possible only after a physical inspection of the vehicle in the workshop.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: => if the DFA wishes to seek even greater transparency for consumers in these cases too, we would point out that a transparent process in which the customer is informed of and agrees to the scope of the order and any necessary adjustments to the order, as has been done so far in our businesses, after receiving the estimate and following the physical diagnosis, can only be regarded as a sign of genuineness and not as a misleading presentation of prices within the meaning of the DFA. The DFA should lay down practical arrangements for this purpose.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: ZDK’s claims

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: In order to achieve digital fairness without damaging the economic foundations of our business, the following points to be taken into account appear to us to be essential:

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: a) Consistent application of the “Think Small First” principle (priority for SMEs)

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: All measures proposed to date and in the future under the DFA must be subject to a rigorous SME compliance test, reflecting the real financial and administrative burdens and their practical feasibility for typical motor vehicle operations.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: ↑↑↑ suggest that exemptions (de minimis rules) for SMEs should be considered.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: b) Clarity and legal certainty before general prohibitions

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: As a basis for action, the Commission would be very helpful in developing and implementing clear and unambiguous definitions and practical guidelines (e.g. positive/negative lists for prohibited practices).

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: c) Recognition and anchoring of B2B fairness as a prerequisite for B2C fairness

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: We consider it relevant to enshrine in the DFA the principle that fair competition vis-à-vis the consumer as a final customer is possible and achievable only if free repairers have non-discriminatory access to data, platforms and technical information. The DFA must actively support the objectives of the Data Act. The above is a prerequisite for the freedom of choice and fair treatment of consumers.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: d) Avoiding double regulation

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The DFA should clarify and complement existing legal acts (in particular the UCPD, GDPR) in a consistent manner and should not lead to conflicting obligations or double regulation of the same facts. The principle of lex specialis should be respected.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: e) Reducing red tape and focusing on enforcement of existing law

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: It is desirable that the consistent application and enforcement of existing rules in the Member States should be a priority over the creation of new laws.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The specific characteristics of trade in high-value and complex goods such as vehicles must be taken into account when designing the rules, in particular with regard to the right of withdrawal, the information requirements and the repair business (cost estimates, service contracts).

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: We are ready to bring our expertise forward in a constructive dialogue with the Commission in order to shape a truly fair and workable legal framework.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: Central Association of German Motor Vehicle Industry (ZDK)

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The ZDK represents the professional interests of all car businesses (car stores and workshops) and is accompanying them by the economic, technical and digital transformation. The ZDK ensures the presence of the motor vehicle industry in the nationwide public and is in constant dialogue with federal ministries, federal authorities, the Bundestag and Bundesrat, as well as decision-makers of associations and institutions, in Bonn and through the capital representative office in Berlin. He also represents his member interests at European level through a dedicated ZDK office in Brussels and membership of the Alliance of European Car Dealers and Repairers (AECDR).

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The motor vehicle industry in Germany: Around 40.000 car houses, car bodies and car repairers, with 468.000 employees. Under the umbrella of the ZDK, 14 regional associations have 234 insertions, together with 34 manufacturers' associations and the Central Association of Body and Vehicle Engineering. Car houses and garages represent around 98.000 apprentices each year in Germany, with a turnover of EUR 224 billion from the sale of new and second-hand vehicles, maintenance, repair and service. Motor vehicle trade is therefore an important economic factor and plays a major role in the transformation of mobility in Germany.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
paragraphs.text: The ZDK is a registered interest representative in the lobby register with No 001246.

section_path: Opinion on the Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (Digital Fairness Act)
Three practical examples:
